results.possibleIdentity: Michael Smith (born 1951) is an American performance, video, and installation artist who emerged as a significant figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1970s. Born in Chicago, he was active in the early 1970s as a student at the Whitney Museum of American Art Independent Study Program (ISP), which he attended in 1970 and 1973. He is most famous for his eponymous performance persona, 'Mike,' an earnest but often unsuccessful everyman, as well as 'Baby Ikki,' an oversized infant character. Smith's work frequently explores the intersection of art, technology, and popular culture, often using humor and satire to examine consumerism and media. He has a long-standing association with the legacy of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the organization founded by Robert Rauschenberg and Billy Klüver. His work is archived and distributed by ArtPix, which also documents seminal E.A.T. projects like '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering.' Additionally, Smith has frequently collaborated with Joshua White, the creator of the 'Joshua Light Show' (active at the Fillmore East in the late 1960s), and was included in the 1979 'Sound' exhibition at MoMA PS1, which featured audio works by artists associated with the E.A.T. Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Expo in Osaka.

Michael Smith (born 1935) is an American playwright and influential theater critic who was a central figure in the New York avant-garde and Off-Off-Broadway movement of the 1960s and 1970s. As the primary drama critic for The Village Voice during the mid-to-late 1960s, he documented many of the formative events of the performance art movement, including the landmark 1966 series '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering,' which served as the public debut for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Beyond his work as a critic, Smith was an active artist in his own right, writing and directing plays for innovative venues like Caffe Cino, La MaMa, and the Open Theatre. His career has spanned decades, and he is recognized as a primary witness and historical documentarian of the collaborative efforts between artists and engineers promoted by E.A.T. in its early years.

results.possibleIdentity: George Gorman was an artist, media theorist, and technical collaborator active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a significant figure in the early video art movement and the New York technological art milieu. Gorman was a member of the Raindance Foundation, an influential video collective founded in 1969, and a contributor to its pioneering journal, "Radical Software" (active 1970–1974). He is frequently associated with the video artist Paul Ryan, collaborating on projects like the "Earthscore Method," which explored cybernetics and environmental perception. Gorman also served as a technical and professional resource within this community; he was involved with Du Art Film Labs in New York, a vital facility for independent and experimental filmmakers of the era. His work and contributions are often cited alongside prominent figures of the time such as John Brockman, Gary Hill, and the Very Reverend James Parks Morton, as part of the cultural and technological shift that informed the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. He has also been credited as a 'storyteller' and writer, exploring the role of narrative and silence in modern art.

Gregory Gorman (frequently known as Greg Gorman) is a prominent American photographer born in 1946 who began his professional career in 1968. He is widely recognized for his high-contrast black-and-white celebrity portraits and artistic nude photography. Gorman's career aligns with the specified timeline, having gained prominence in the late 1960s and early 1970s after photographing Jimi Hendrix in 1968. While primarily known for his commercial and portrait work, his artistic career has spanned over five decades, and he has collaborated with numerous artists and cultural figures in New York and Los Angeles, environments where Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was active. However, his work is more traditionally categorized as photography rather than the collaborative technical experimentation typical of E.A.T.
